items.q: An object moves in a circle at constant speed. Is it accelerating?
items.correct: Yes — its direction is constantly changing, so its velocity changes; centripetal acceleration points toward the centre of the circle.
items.misconception: No — constant speed means no acceleration.
items.random_wrong: Only if it speeds up or slows down on the circle.
items.why: Students equate acceleration with speeding up, missing that any change in velocity (including direction) constitutes acceleration.

items.q: Why is gravitational potential energy near a planet treated as negative?
items.correct: Because the zero is defined at infinity; objects bound to the planet have lower (negative) potential energy than at infinity.
items.misconception: Gravitational potential energy is negative because gravity always points downwards.
items.random_wrong: Gravitational potential energy is negative because gravity does negative work.
items.why: Students confuse the negative sign as 'direction' rather than understanding it as a consequence of choosing infinity as the zero point.

items.q: For a projectile launched horizontally, what affects the horizontal component of velocity during flight (ignoring air resistance)?
items.correct: Nothing — horizontal velocity stays constant; only vertical velocity changes due to gravity.
items.misconception: Gravity slows down both the vertical and horizontal motion.
items.random_wrong: The horizontal velocity increases over time because the projectile is falling.
items.why: Students treat 'gravity affects all motion' too broadly, missing that gravity acts only vertically and the components are independent.

items.q: An ideal gas at constant volume has its absolute temperature doubled. Its pressure:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Doubles. “Halves” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: pV = constant. “V/T = constant” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
